subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.1.1: Demonstrate understanding of a variety of songs
indicator_description: Explain the central messages in songs
examplars:
1. Have learners sing some familiar patriotic songs.
2. Have learners listen to you sing a selected song.
3. Guide learners to sing lines of the song with appropriate stress and rhythm.
4. Let them sing individually and in groups.
5. Using questions discuss the central message of the target song: Who composed this song? What is the song telling us?
6. Invite individuals to explain the central message(s) of the song.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.1.1: Demonstrate understanding of a variety of songs
indicator_description: Relate values in songs to real life experiences
examplars:
1. Have learners sing familiar songs as in the previous lesson.
2. Lead learners to discuss the song and have them bring out the meaning.
3. In groups, learners discuss the values in the song, e.g. truthfulness, patience, etc. and relate the values to real life situations.
4. Have groups share their ideas with the class in the form of presentations.
5. Write salient responses on the chalkboard.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.3.1: Appreciate poems and other pieces of literary materials
indicator_description: Respond to poems by discussing their central messages and expressing own opinion
examplars:
1. Ask learners to recite a poem of their choice with actions.
2. Choose a poem appropriate for the grade level.
3. Recite and act out the poem as learners listen attentively to you and observe.
4. Allow some individual learners to recite as others listen.
5. Lead learners to recite lines of the poem with correct stress, clapping and tapping out the rhythm.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.3.1: Appreciate poems and other pieces of literary materials
indicator_description: Relate values in poems to day-to-day life
examplars:
1. Ask learners to recite some poems already learnt. Write out a new poem on the board. Teach the meaning and pronunciation of the key words in context.
2. Lead learners to read the poem line by line and discuss with them the meaning of the whole poem.
3. Recite the poem with pupils line by line, clapping and tapping out the rhythm.
4. Together with learners identify and discuss some values in the poem, e.g. patience, love, patriotism etc.
5. Have learners relate the values to real life situations and experiences.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.3.1: Appreciate poems and other pieces of literary materials
indicator_description: Compose six-line poems.
examplars:
1. Have learners recite a poem of their choice individually and in groups.
2. Put learners into groups and ask them to write out some lines of the recited poems.
3. Assign learners to create or write some meaningful lines similar to those in the poem of their choice.
4. Guide learners to arrange their formulated lines together to compose a poem of six lines.
5. Have each group recite their poem while others listen.
6. Ask the class to identify the values in the poems as they listen to recitations.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.4.1: Respond to stories
indicator_description: Demonstrate understanding of lessons in stories by making relevant comments
examplars:
1. Tell a story using the appropriate procedure or stages. With examples, guide learners to identify the beginning, middle and ending.
2. Invite learners to retell story sequentially.
3. Story Element: What are the key details about this story? Who is the main character? What is the setting? What event(s) happened at the beginning of the story? What event(s) happened at the middle of the story? What event(s) happened at the end of the story?
4. Have learners take turns to comment on the story.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.4.1: Respond to stories
indicator_description: Tell stories which are parallel to stories heard or read
examplars:
1. Use an example to explain a parallel poem or story.
2. Lead the class to write a story parallel to a familiar story.
3. Present another short story on the board.
4. Elicit and discuss areas that can be changed or replaced to create a parallel story.
5. Assign groups to write and present stories parallel to that on the board.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.5.1: Appreciate pieces of literary materials through dramatisation
indicator_description: Use costume to dramatise or role-play parts/whole of stories
examplars:
1. Lead learners to select a familiar story for dramatisation.
2. Ask questions to review the story.
3. Assign groups to prepare (share roles, select costume and rehearse) and dramatise/role-play the story using improvised lines.
4. Invite groups to perform for the class to critique their performances.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.5.2: Appreciate key issues in stories/sketches
indicator_description: Interpret moral values in plays/stories
examplars:
1. Select stories or plays recently watched or read by learners.
2. Use questions to guide learners to identify the moral values (e. g. truthfulness, perseverance, etc.) in the story/play.
3. Put learners in groups to interpret the moral values identified and share their interpretation with the class.
4. Have learners discuss how to apply those values to day-to-day living.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.5.2: Appreciate key issues in stories/sketches
indicator_description: Develop sketches from stories read or heard
examplars:
1. Through discussion, guide learners to identify the parts in stories read.
2. Invite some learners to retell parts of a story read or heard.
3. Discuss the story with learners.
4. Use the activity to guide learners in groups to develop a sketch from the story.
5. Have learners read out their sketches to the class for comments.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.5.2: Appreciate key issues in stories/sketches
indicator_description: Analyse the actions of characters in sketches
examplars:
1. Sing songs that accompany story telling with learners.
2. Choose familiar stories from learners' background.
3. Put learners into groups. Revise the previous activity on writing short sketches. Learners sketch a story told.
4. Through questions guide learners to identify and analyse characters and their actions in their sketches.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Conversation - Talking about Oneself, Family, People, Customs, Social/Cultural Values and Manners
content_standard: B5.1.6.1: Use culturally acceptable language for communication
indicator_description: Describe/talk about names of regions/places in the country as well as personalities in the community, expressing ideas and feelings clearly
examplars:
1. Note: Items in this indicator should be treated in separate lessons. The procedure below may be useful.
2. Discuss some important personalities in the community.
3. Ask pupils to mention the regions in the country.
4. In groups, have learners talk about important places in each region.
5. Have groups read their findings to the class.
6. Encourage the class to ask questions for clarification of ideas

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Conversation - Talking about Oneself, Family, People, Customs, Social/Cultural Values and Manners
content_standard: B5.1.6.2: Demonstrate positive listening and viewing attitudes and behaviour by showing attentiveness and understanding
indicator_description: Maintain appropriate posture and facial expression
examplars:
1. Discuss with learners the importance of appropriate posture and facial expression in conversation.
2. Learners in groups choose a theme and engage in conversation maintaining appropriate posture and facial expressions.
3. Learners listen and observe the speaker's use of these skills and later critique to help them acquire or improve them.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Conversation - Talking about Oneself, Family, People, Customs, Social/Cultural Values and Manners
content_standard: B5.1.6.2: Demonstrate positive listening and viewing attitudes and behaviour by showing attentiveness and understanding
indicator_description: Listen and view for the entire duration of a text, speech, presentation, video etc.
examplars:
1. Play an audio video on your phone or laptop and let learners listen carefully. Set a task to guide them to focus on the activity.
2. Discuss what they saw and heard. Learners in their groups describe what they saw and heard and do a presentation.
3. Select a real life scenario e.g. head teacher addressing the school after winning the sports competition. Learners in groups organise their speech; elect a representative to read while others listen.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Conversation - Talking about Oneself, Family, People, Customs, Social/Cultural Values and Manners
content_standard: B5.1.6.3: Use knowledge of language and communicative skills to participate in conversation
indicator_description: Engage in collaborative conversation on topics such as social issues, values and manners with adults
examplars:
1. Show the video of a talk show or introduce a talk show by drawing on learners background knowledge of such activities on TV.
2. Identify an interesting topic e.g. "Children should not WhatsApp". Initiate a conversation using questions. Learners take turns to contribute to the conversation.
3. Put learners in groups and let them choose a topic for conversation. Go round to ensure learners take turns, use facial expressions appropriately and maintain eye contact when in conversation.
4. Put learners in groups to identify and discuss social values (e. g. honesty), what they are and behaviours that portray these values.
5. They should identify people they know who have exhibited these values and how they showed these values.
6. Have each group present their work to the class. Encourage the class to ask questions and contribute to the presentation.
7. Repeat the procedure to help learners to talk about manners.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Conversation - Talking about Oneself, Family, People, Customs, Social/Cultural Values and Manners
content_standard: B5.1.6.3: Use knowledge of language and communicative skills to participate in conversation
indicator_description: Demonstrate turn taking in conversation in different topics and follow agreed-upon rules for conversation, e.g. listening to others, speaking one at a time.
examplars:
1. Through discussion, guide learners to identify some events that happened in the day or recently.
2. Invite some individual learners to discuss with the class some experiences. e.g. The learner was late and had to help somebody on the way to school.
3. Have a pair of learners converse on a given topic (e.g. a football match) as others watch.
4. Learners in pairs then converse, talking about different topics after the example presented.
5. Guide the use of appropriate vocabulary by showing vocabulary cards and indirectly dropping hints of a correct word.
6. Encourage learners to take turns in the activity. e.g. What did you just say? Say that again. Can you repeat what you said, sorry, excuse me.
7. Learners demonstrate the use of this in pairs in front of the class.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Conversation - Talking about Oneself, Family, People, Customs, Social/Cultural Values and Manners
content_standard: B5.1.6.3: Use knowledge of language and communicative skills to participate in conversation
indicator_description: Respond to questions to give further clarification of an opinion
examplars:
1. Revise the activity on the talk show. Learners give examples of some vocabulary learnt.
2. Together with learners, choose a topic and initiate a conversation. Encourage learners to ask questions for clarification, and use appropriate expressions.
3. Put pupils into groups. Have each group choose a theme and initiate a conversation on their theme. Have the others listen and ask questions.
4. Have the group respond to the questions to give further clarifications

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.7.1: Use appropriate skills and strategies to process meaning from texts
indicator_description: Employ the Think-Aloud strategy to convey meaning from level-appropriate texts.
examplars:
1. Have learners listen to an audio text or a teacher-read text.
2. Model Think-Aloud strategy to make meaning from the text.
3. Have learners practise the Think-Aloud strategy to construct meaning.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.7.1: Use appropriate skills and strategies to process meaning from texts
indicator_description: Make connections with events in drama
examplars:
1. Have learners listen to an audio drama or watch a video.
2. Guide learners to identify the sequence of events in the story, i.e. events at the beginning, middle and ending.
3. Ask relevant questions to guide learners to relate the events in the drama to their lives.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.7.1: Use appropriate skills and strategies to process meaning from texts
indicator_description: Relate to lessons in stories
examplars:
1. Assist learners to revise the sequence of a story familiar to them.
2. Have learners identify the characters in the story and the roles the characters played in the story or text.
3. Learners again identify the setting of the story.
4. Learners identify the moral lessons from the story.
5. Help them discuss and relate the moral lessons to their lives using an example to illustrate that.
6. Read a short story to learners and in groups, have them relate to the lessons in the story by repeating the above activities.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.7.1: Use appropriate skills and strategies to process meaning from texts
indicator_description: Use background knowledge to understand and build new knowledge while listening to drama
examplars:
1. Have learners listen to a drama.
2. Learners in groups identify, discuss and relate the plot of the drama to a familiar text.
3. Have learners listen to the drama a second time.
4. In groups, learners share what they have learnt in the text.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.7.1: Use appropriate skills and strategies to process meaning from texts
indicator_description: Ask relevant questions to expand comprehension of details of texts
examplars:
1. Through discussion, learners identify some interesting stories or texts they have heard or read.
2. Assist learners to talk about the content of the stories/texts after re-telling the story.
3. Encourage learners to ask relevant questions to expand their comprehension of the details of the text.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.7.1: Use appropriate skills and strategies to process meaning from texts
indicator_description: Distinguish between causes and effects of events in a story
examplars:
1. Select an appropriate story to be read in class.
2. Have learners read the story and re-tell it to the class.
3. Work together with learners to identify the events in the story and link them sequentially by identifying cause and effect.
4. Learners in their groups choose stories and identify the causes and effects of events. Let learners present their work as the rest listen to them.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.7.1: Use appropriate skills and strategies to process meaning from texts
indicator_description: Draw conclusions from main ideas, key details and specific examples from texts
examplars:
1. Revise the lessons on identification of cause and effect using Think-Pair-Share.
2. Select an interesting story to guide the learners do this exercise.
3. Build on this activity by working together with learners to identify specific examples to help bring out main ideas and the key details. With this example, guide learners to draw conclusions.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Asking and Answering Questions/ Question Tags
content_standard: B5.1.8.1: Demonstrate understanding in asking and answering questions correctly
indicator_description: Use the various forms of "do", "be", "have", appropriately in questions and responses
examplars:
1. Revise Wh and Yes/No questions by having learners respond to several of such questions.
2. Use the various forms of "do" to construct sentences as learners listen to you. e. g. Do you like lots of pepper? Does he teach here? Did we sleep there?
3. Answer each question yourself.
4. Ask the questions again and have learners give the answer.
5. In pairs, learners ask questions for their partners to answer.
6. Repeat the procedure to introduce the various forms of "be" (is, am, are etc.) and "have" (has, have, had).

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Asking and Answering Questions/ Question Tags
content_standard: B5.1.8.2: Identify and use question tags correctly in speech
indicator_description: Use positive tags, negative tags and auxiliaries in speech
examplars:
1. Play a recorded dialogue containing positive tags or get two learners to engage in a dialogue prepared by the teacher containing positive tags.
2. Learners listen and in pairs, use positive tags in dialogues. Do the same with negative tags. Go through the same procedure to get learners to use the tags in speech. (Explain that question tags are short questions that follow statements).
3. Together with learners, discuss the formation of tags by listening to and practising using them in speech. e.g. The bad boys aren't in school today, are they?
4. In a Question and Answer Drill, pairs of learners ask and answer questions round the class.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Giving and Following Commands/ Instructions/Directions and Making and Responding to Requests
content_standard: B5.1.9.1: Use verbs in commands, instructions, requests and directions appropriately in speech
indicator_description: Give and respond to commands, instructions; give and follow directions with clear landmarks
examplars:
1. Revise the concept of giving commands and making requests.
2. In pairs, have learners role-play giving and obeying or following commands/instructions.
3. Model giving simple directions to places in the school and important places in the community or environment.
4. Provide situations for learners to practise giving directions. E.g. "A stranger meets you at the school gate. Direct him or her to the chief's palace, Central Mosque etc.
5. Learners work in groups to give directions.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Giving and Following Commands/ Instructions/Directions and Making and Responding to Requests
content_standard: B5.1.9.1: Use verbs in commands, instructions, requests and directions appropriately in speech
indicator_description: Make and respond to polite requests for help from familiar adults
examplars:
1. Revise how to make requests by giving examples.
2. In a discussion, help learners to identify the need to use "please" when making simple requests. Through demonstration, guide learners to make polite requests in the classroom. e.g. i. Please sir/madam, may I go out? ii. Yes, you may.
3. Invite learners to the front in pairs to dramatise/role-play situations/scenes where they make requests and respond to them. Provide and teach vocabulary and expressions to guide them make requests and respond to them.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.1: Demonstrate knowledge of spoken grammar and register
indicator_description: Demonstrate awareness of the features of spoken language, e.g. use of discourse markers – well, also, finally
examplars:
1. Revise important features of spoken language such as use of contractions and ellipsis.
2. Help learners to use contractions and ellipsis in speech. E.g. 1. Contraction: i. Kofi cannot come today. ii. Kofi can't come today. 2. Ellipis: A: When will you come? B: Tomorrow

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.1: Demonstrate knowledge of spoken grammar and register
indicator_description: Demonstrate awareness of the differences between spoken and written forms of language, e.g. formal and informal register
examplars:
1. Revise some differences between spoken and written forms of language, e.g. simplicity in spoken sentences and complexity of written sentences. e.g. written: I have not gone there. spoken: I haven't gone there.
2. Demonstrate simplicity of spoken language by giving short and simple speeches on a topic. E.g. Introduction of a chairman.
3. Encourage learners to give short and simple speeches on chosen topics, e.g. My Favourite Meal.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.1: Demonstrate knowledge of spoken grammar and register
indicator_description: Demonstrate awareness of how meaning is conveyed through appropriate pace, stress, tone – through reciting poems.
examplars:
1. Perform a familiar poem as learners listen and observe.
2. Have learners discuss the recital with focus on intonation, stress and rhythm.
3. Perform it again and have the class or groups do same.
4. Individuals, pairs and groups now choose and perform their favourite poems.
5. Have the class critique each effort.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.2: Demonstrate the ability to communicate with accurate pronunciation and appropriate intonation
indicator_description: Speak audibly and coherently, using the appropriate voice qualities (pace, tone etc.)
examplars:
1. Have learners listen to a conversation or speech in which the speaker speaks fluently and clearly and another audio where the speech is neither clear nor fluent.
2. Discuss the differences and ask learners to tell the importance of clear speech.
3. Create situations for learners to engage in dialogue (a pair at a time) or speech delivery.
4. Encourage them to maintain a good pace and tone.
5. Invite comments from the class after each delivery.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.2: Demonstrate the ability to communicate with accurate pronunciation and appropriate intonation
indicator_description: Read aloud clearly, at a good pace and with expression
examplars:
1. Have learners listen to audio recordings of speeches or listen to news read in English.
2. Learners listen to the speech in meaningful bits to guide them identify appropriate expressions as well as identify what a good pace is.
3. Use further samples to show that good speeches are presented with the appropriate expression and at a good pace.
4. In groups learners pick topics for their speeches. They prepare and deliver in turns. Let them deliver the speeches in about five (5) minutes each.
5. Have the class discuss the pace and use of appropriate expressions.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.3: Plan and present information and ideas for a variety of purposes
indicator_description: Present information about personal experiences
examplars:
1. Model presenting a speech on a topic or have them listen to a recording of someone's personal experience. Use the example to guide learners to identify the (purpose of and audience for the speech.)
2. Discuss the importance of purpose and audience in speech.
3. Put learners in groups to plan a parallel speech and present to the class. Each group should select a purpose for and an audience for their speech.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.3: Plan and present information and ideas for a variety of purposes
indicator_description: Draw on prior knowledge to identify organisational structure of speech
examplars:
1. Have learners listen to a speech and give the written text to them to read taking note of the features.
2. Discuss the structure of a model speech with learners: e.g. i. Heading ii. Vocative Address iii. Introduction iv. Body
3. In groups have pupils write and present a parallel speech. Have the class critique each group's work based on the discussed structures.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.3: Plan and present information and ideas for a variety of purposes
indicator_description: Gather and select facts and ideas from one or multiple print and non-print sources, appropriate to the purpose, audience, context and culture
examplars:
1. Review the organizational structure of the speech with pupils by giving a short speech and asking them to identify the various structures.
2. Provide sample speeches for discussion to help learners generate ideas for their speeches.
3. Use the internet to guide the learners to download important speeches.
4. Together with learners, discuss the organisational structure of the speeches.
5. Have learners choose and write speeches.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.3: Plan and present information and ideas for a variety of purposes
indicator_description: Support ideas and points of view by integrating selected visual and audio resources, verbal and non-verbal cues (e.g. gestures, facial expressions)
examplars:
1. Through discussion, guide learners to select facts and ideas from one or more sources such as print sources and non print sources (e.g. resource person) appropriate to the purpose, audience, context and culture.
2. Encourage learners to ask questions for clarity.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.3: Plan and present information and ideas for a variety of purposes
indicator_description: Use discourse markers to signpost stages in a presentation (e.g. "For the next part", "For example", "In summary")
examplars:
1. Select a sample written speech and through discussion help learners to identify discourse markers to signpost stages in a presentation. E.g. "Let us look at," also, " To sum up," "Finally".
2. Learners write and use the appropriate words in both formal and informal situations.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.3: Plan and present information and ideas for a variety of purposes
indicator_description: Elaborate on points using concrete examples
examplars:
1. Through model discussion help learners to elaborate on their points and substantiate them through the use of details, concrete examples, experiences and feelings.
2. Have learners present their works to the hearing of others.
3. Encourage the class to make inputs in one another's work.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.1.10.3: Plan and present information and ideas for a variety of purposes
indicator_description: Speak with confidence before different audiences and maintain appropriate posture
examplars:
1. Through role play and frequent practice in presentation, build confidence in learners to enable them stand before different audiences.
2. Let them demonstrate various postures, maintain eye contact and use verbal and non-verbal signs to convey meaning. Let them practice these in pairs and groups.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.2.1: Connect sounds to letters; and blend letters/syllables in order to read and write
indicator_description: Apply common phonic generalisations (e.g. hard and soft "c" and "g") when reading continuous texts.
examplars:
1. e.g. "c" as in country and cell; "g" as in gain and age.
2. Review the hard form of the sound, e.g. "c" as in cut; "g" as in get.
3. Introduce the soft form using lots of examples, e.g. "c" as in centre, cent, circle; "g" as in gent, gin, etc.
4. In groups, encourage learners to come out with words in which the sounds occur.
5. Have each group read their words to the class and then publish it on the walls of the classroom for learners to go round to read.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.2.1: Connect sounds to letters; and blend letters/syllables in order to read and write
indicator_description: Read two syllable words with suffixes and "r" controlled words (or, er, ar , ur) when reading continuous texts.
examplars:
1. Introduce learners to the "r" controlled words. e.g. or as in lord, ford; er as in her, fern, term, herb; ar as in arm, car, park; ur as in burn, turn, purse.
2. Invite learners to give their examples.
3. Put learners into groups and assign each group sounds. Using a dictionary and the internet, have members of each group search for words that contain the sound.
4. Have each group read their words to the class.
5. Get learners to read two syllable words that contain suffixes and "r" controlled words (e.g. return) in sentences.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Word Families: Minimal Pairs and Common Digraphs
content_standard: B5.2.3.1: Identify minimal pairs and common digraphs
indicator_description: Use common minimal pairs to decode words. e.g. - sash, wash
examplars:
1. Give examples of minimal pairs, e.g. watch, catch.
2. In groups let learners come up with more examples.
3. Select two paragraphs from the reading passage and let learners identify specific spelling patterns e.g. sh- fish, dish, sash, wash; th - both, tooth.
4. Drill learners on pronunciation of words.
5. Learners read the words containing minimal pairs, e.g. church, search, perch, fetch.
6. Work together with learners to write simple sentences with the minimal pairs.
7. Learners in pairs identify more words from reading passages and make a list on a chart. Let each pair read the words.

subject: English Language
sub_strand: Word Families: Minimal Pairs and Common Digraphs
content_standard: B5.2.3.1: Identify minimal pairs and common digraphs
indicator_description: Use words with digraphs to make meaningful sentences
examplars:
1. Revise the activity in B5.2.3.1.1 and show the chart containing words. Let learners think-pair-share and select a paragraph with words containing digraphs e.g. wh, th, both at initial and end positions e.g. that, those, these, with, sixth, teeth.
2. Put learners into groups. Let them identify and read words containing digraphs and use them in sentences.
3. The group that produces ten words and uses them in sentences correctly is the winner.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.4.1: Identify and use diphthongs to decode
indicator_description: Use closing diphthongs, e.g. /aƱ/, /eƱ/to make meaningful sentences
examplars:
1. Revise the activity on minimal pairs words. Let learners pick partners, think pair-share. Learners use their rhyming words in meaningful sentences.
2. Introduce words that contain the target diphthongs, one at a time, by writing examples on the board e.g. /eƱ/ - go, no, boat, load etc.; /aƱ/ - how, fowl etc. Note: Do not write the symbols of the sounds.
3. Learners read and identify the common sound in the words. In groups learners make a list of words containing diphthongs and use some in sentences e.g. she says today is pay day.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.5.1: Identify and use consonant blends and clusters in reading
indicator_description: Orally produce two-syllable words by blending sounds (phonemes), including consonant blends
examplars:
1. Learners individually make meaningful sentences with words containing diphthongs. Every correct sentence is recorded for the group the individual belongs to.
2. Introduce two-syllable words with consonant blends.
3. Model the pronunciation and have learners say them after you.
4. Teacher and learners select a reading text to guide the class to identify words containing consonant blends.
5. List words on the board and drill learners in their pronunciation.
6. Learners read sentences and pick out the two-syllable words with consonant blends. e.g. dr-hundred, dr-drumstick, bl-problem, nd-handcuff.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.5.1: Identify and use consonant blends and clusters in reading
indicator_description: Use the spelling-sound correspondences for common consonant digraphs
examplars:
1. Revise digraphs. Have a simple presentation of digraphs by writing examples of words that begin or end with them on the board.
2. Have learners read the words and identify the letters at the beginning and the end. e.g. taught , watch, splash, photographs.
3. Together with learners use the internet to identify words containing specific digraphs.
4. Learners write examples of words, find their meanings and use them in sentences. E.g. lt- belt, fr- fresh, pl- play.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.6.1: Understand word meanings and usages
indicator_description: Use level-appropriate content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives and adverbs), and function words (e.g. prepositions) appropriately in spoken and written communication
examplars:
1. Explain to learners that function words glue pieces of sentences together into long patterns. In other words they express a grammatical or structural relationship with other words in a sentence.
2. Examples of function words are determiners, conjunctions, prepositions, auxiliary verbs, modals etc.
3. Content words are words with specific meanings such as nouns, adjectives, adverbs and main verbs. e.g. The sly brown fox jumped over the lazy dog.
4. In groups learners identify and work out the meanings of content words in texts read.
5. Have them use these words in sentences.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.6.1: Understand word meanings and usages
indicator_description: Use the following terms: synonym, antonym, prefix, suffix, phrasal verb etc. in spoken and written expressions.
examplars:
1. Choose a text to guide learners identify synonyms, antonyms, idioms or phrasal verbs etc.
2. Create a scenario where learners can use idioms, antonyms, prefixes, suffixes and phrasal verbs, synonyms, etc. in meaningful sentences in written and spoken languages.
core_competencies: DL, Listening and Speaking, Reading and Writing

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.6.2: Build vocabulary
indicator_description: Develop a rich vocabulary stock through extensive reading of age-appropriate texts, using the dictionary or online resources to look up meanings of words etc.
examplars:
1. Choose and read an age-appropriate story to the class.
2. Have learners talk about the story.
3. Let them identify unfamiliar vocabulary and find their meanings in context.
4. Have learners use the Build My Vocab App on the internet to help build the most globally used language to help develop their vocabulary.
5. Guide learners to choose books from the school library or distribute supplementary readers to them.
6. Introduce a simple vocabulary book made by teacher to guide the learners build their vocabulary.
7. Read to learners to encourage them read extensively. Encourage individual learners to read their selected story books to the class.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.6.3: Demonstrate a rich vocabulary that supports the development of listening, reading, speaking, writing and presentation skills
indicator_description: Deduce meaning of words from the word class they belong to and how they relate to one another (synonyms and antonyms).
examplars:
1. Teach meaning of keywords in a text by using them in sentences.
2. In groups of six have learners bring out the contextual meaning of the words using synonyms and antonyms.
3. Have learners use these words in their own sentences.
4. Have them present their work to the class for feedback.
5. Have learners play vocabulary games, e.g. The Synonym/Antonym Tree game.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.6.4: Use words appropriately for purpose, audience, context and culture
indicator_description: Recognise how words are formed through acronyms (WHO) and clipping (telephone–phone).
examplars:
1. Have learners revise building vocabulary through affixes. e.g. micro – microscope; in – insert; con – connect; re – return.
2. Learners should find meaning to these affixes and write more examples of affixes. Play a game where learners identify affixes in words. The group with the highest number of words is declared the winner.
3. Introduce acronyms and clipping. e.g.: acronyms – WHO = World Health Organisation; clipping – telephone = phone.
4. Put learners into groups and have them research on acronyms and clipping, using dictionaries or online resources, to build more vocabulary.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.6.4: Use words appropriately for purpose, audience, context and culture
indicator_description: Recognise the playful use of words in spoken and written language (jokes, riddles, puns etc.)
examplars:
1. Demonstrate the playful use of words (jokes, riddles etc.)
2. e.g. Riddle, riddle. I am something. I have four legs and a top. People write or eat on me. What am I?
3. Have learners use appropriate language in jokes, riddles etc.
4. Make them understand that these are ways in which language could also be used.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.6.4: Use words appropriately for purpose, audience, context and culture
indicator_description: Use words suitable for purpose, audience, context and culture in relation to: type of texts (expository/explanatory, persuasive language, argumentative), medium (spoken/written), register (formal/informal) etc.
examplars:
1. Demonstrate the use of the key elements in writing. E.g. purpose, audience, setting, characters etc.
2. Have learners to role-play a simple story to bring out the importance of these elements and their usage.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.7.1: Process and comprehend level appropriate texts
indicator_description: Construct meaning from text read
examplars: 1. Choose different texts for learners to make meaning from using the different strategies they are familiar with: - Use of prior knowledge/experience. - Making predictions - Contextual meaning of vocabulary to connect with the other words to make meaning.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.7.1: Process and comprehend level appropriate texts
indicator_description: Note and recall main ideas in a sequence
examplars: 1. Using SQ3R guide learners to present facts and ideas in a sequential order.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.7.1: Process and comprehend level appropriate texts
indicator_description: Scan texts for details
examplars:
1. Guide learners to skim and scan texts for details.
2. Have learners skim/scan a text in a short period/time frame and write down their ideas.
3. Discuss the answers together as a class.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.7.1: Process and comprehend level appropriate texts
indicator_description: Read level-appropriate texts silently and closely for comprehension.
examplars:
1. Revise the Before Reading, During Reading and After Reading strategies introduced to learners.
2. Provide appropriate texts and ensure that the strategies are used.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.7.2: Apply critical reading, implied meaning, higher order thinking, judgment and evaluation
indicator_description: Respond to a text with simple judgment
examplars:
1. Use appropriate questions to guide learners read, identify and present points of view in a text.
2. Learners create a mental image from the text read visualising/ bringing the text to life by engaging the imagination and using all the senses e.g. use questions to guide them do this activity. Can you describe pictures or images you made in your head when you read the text?
3. Through relevant questions, have learners respond to the text with simple judgment. Learners should present their points in written or oral form.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.7.2: Apply critical reading, implied meaning, higher order thinking, judgment and evaluation
indicator_description: Relate two or more ideas in a text
examplars:
1. Through relevant questions, have learners make personal connections with a text read. e.g. Does the message in this passage remind you of something? Answer: This story reminds me of a holiday I spent with my grandfather.
2. Have learners relate two or more ideas within the text or from different texts.
core_competencies: CC, Reading and Writing, CP, Listening and Speaking

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.7.2: Apply critical reading, implied meaning, higher order thinking, judgment and evaluation
indicator_description: Demonstrate awareness of the structure of texts (e.g. introduction, body, conclusion)
examplars:
1. Guide learners with questions to identify the main parts of a story, e.g. introduction, body and conclusion.
2. Have learners use connectives to summarise each part of the text and use these to make a full summary.
core_competencies: Listening and Speaking, Reading and Writing

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.7.3: Demonstrate an understanding of the use of words and phrases as used in a text
indicator_description: Use knowledge of prefixes and suffixes to read and interpret unfamiliar words. e.g. mis – misunderstanding
examplars:
1. Revise affixes briefly and have learners identify them in words.
2. Have learners use words in their own sentences

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.8.1: Construct meaning from texts read
indicator_description: Infer meaning from level-appropriate texts
examplars:
1. Have learners read silently. Let them stop and think about something different from the text. This is to make them know what to do when meaning is disrupted.
2. Have them read silently for the meaning from the text.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.8.1: Construct meaning from texts read
indicator_description: Find the meaning of words as used in context
examplars:
1. Use examples to show that words have different meanings.
2. Have a vocabulary lesson to help learners find meaning of words in a text.
3. Have learners use Think-Pair-Share to discuss their meaning together.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.8.1: Construct meaning from texts read
indicator_description: Answer questions based on the passage read
examplars:
1. Learners pose and answer questions that clarify meaning and promote deeper understanding of a text.
2. Questions can be generated by the learner, a peer or an adult. E.g. i. Why did the character do nothing? ii. How did it make them feel?

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.9.1: Read fluently to enhance comprehension
indicator_description: Read grade-level texts with good speed and accuracy
examplars:
1. Use the three-stage approach to a comprehension lesson and have learners prepare and read the text with purpose and understanding.
2. Use different strategies to assist learners to come up with meaning of a text ,e.g. The Herringbone, KWL etc.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.9.1: Read fluently to enhance comprehension
indicator_description: Use context to confirm or self-correction strategies to confirm understanding of level-appropriate texts
examplars:
1. Have learners read the grade appropriate texts paying attention to punctuation marks, words, sentences and paragraphs.
2. Provide questions that will guide learners in understanding the passage, e.g., What word connects paragraphs one and two?
3. Learners use correction strategy to help them make meaning from the text as they read.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.10.1: Read and summarise passages read
indicator_description: Summarise level-appropriate texts/passages orally
examplars: 1. Learners identify the most important ideas in texts/passages read and restate them in their own words. E.g. i. Can you retell the story? ii. Who are the main characters? iii. What is the setting? iv. What are the main events? (use herringbone or story map as strategies)
core_competencies: CC, PL, Reading, Listening and Speaking

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.2.10.1: Read and summarise passages read
indicator_description: Write a short summary of a level-appropriate text/passage read
examplars:
1. Have learners work in groups to write a summary of a text/passage read taking note of the main ideas.
2. Let learners present their write-up for discussion and feedback from the class and teacher.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.1.1: Apply knowledge of different types of nouns in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use nouns or noun phrases to refer to quantities or units.
examplars:
1. Revise nouns briefly.
2. Introduce quantities and units in context. e.g.: - Please, give me a piece of paper. - I have a pair of trousers.
3. Have learners identify more examples of these in text and use them in sentences.
4. Learners make up their own noun phrases and use them in sentences.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.1.1: Apply knowledge of different types of nouns in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use: proper nouns - refer to festivals; Count/non-count; Singular; Plural (regular, irregular); plural without plural marker
examplars:
1. Proper nouns: Introduce the concept in context. E.g. Christmas is coming. The people of Ga celebrate Homowo. - In pairs have learners give more examples of festivals and write them as proper nouns.
2. Count and non-count nouns: Revise the concept of proper nouns and common nouns. (Proper nouns refer to particular nouns such as names of people, places, mountains, etc.) Elicit examples from learners. Briefly discuss what common nouns are giving and eliciting several examples. Use several examples to explain and exemplify count and non-count nouns and provide activities for practice.
3. Singular and Plural (regular, irregular) nouns: Introduce singular and plural nouns in context. E.g. I need a table. Can you give me one of these tables? This child looks sick, but those children look healthy. Have learners read the sentences and observe the underlined words. Show pictures of objects/people to help learners to identify regular and irregular nouns e.g. picture of a child, and a picture of children. Guide learners to discover the difference between regular and irregular plural forms: - Regular count nouns take s or es to form their plural. e.g. table – tables, chair – chairs - Irregular count nouns are nouns that do not take s or es to form their plural. e.g. child – children; ox – oxen; man – men; goose – geese
4. Plural nouns without plural markers: Have learners discover the plural nouns without plural marker in context. E.g. The sheep are grazing outside. I met a lot of people at the party.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.1.1: Apply knowledge of different types of nouns in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use collective nouns to refer to a group of objects and people
examplars:
1. Teach collective nouns in context.
2. Introduce a text that contains examples of collective nouns. E.g. such as audience, congregation, a flock of sheep, a troop of monkeys.
3. Have learners discuss the meaning of these words in context.
4. Put them into groups to provide more examples of collective nouns and use these nouns in sentences.
5. Let the groups present their work to the class for feedback.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.1.1: Apply knowledge of different types of nouns in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use abstract nouns to refer to concepts and ideas
examplars:
1. Revise concrete nouns.
2. Introduce abstract nouns as nouns that cannot be touched, seen or felt. They include ghost, beauty, holiness, air, etc.
3. Provide a passage with concrete and abstract nouns and let learners identify each category.
4. Have learners use given concrete and abstract nouns in sentences.
core_competencies: CC, PL, Writing, Reading and Writing

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.2.1: Apply knowledge of different types of determiners in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use indefinite and definite articles "a" and "an" to refer to a person, animal, event, time or objects in general
examplars:
1. Provide an appropriate text containing abstract, concrete nouns.
2. Revise the lesson on nouns.
3. Use the same text to introduce definite, indefinites and zero articles.
4. Guide learners with examples to list the articles in the passage.
5. Help learners to form sentences using the articles.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.2.1: Apply knowledge of different types of determiners in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use quantifiers to show qualities: ordinal first, second etc.), a few/a little etc., both each/every, another, other, fewer, less, etc.
examplars:
1. Introduce other types of determiners like each, both, and all.
2. Let learners read the sentences containing these determiners and explain the sentences.
3. Provide a text and let learners identify the determiners in a group activity.
4. Provide a context containing quantifiers. With examples help learners to identify them. e.g. i. Numerals (cardinals – one, two, three); ii. Ordinals (first, second, third); iii. Some few/little etc; iv. No/both, all, each/every.
5. Help learners to use the quantifiers in sentences.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.2.1: Apply knowledge of different types of determiners in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use possessive pronouns to show possession
examplars:
1. Provide an interesting story abundant in possessives, e.g. mine, yours, his. E.g., This is mine.
2. Let learners use possessives like mine, yours, his, hers, theirs in sentences. E.g., This story is mine.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.2.1: Apply knowledge of different types of determiners in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use demonstratives: this/that, these/those people
examplars:
1. Have learners identify demonstrations like this/that, these/those in context.
2. Guide learners with examples to write down the demonstratives.
3. Have learners use them in sentences. E.g. i. This is for John. ii. That is his plan.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.2.1: Apply knowledge of different types of determiners in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use interrogative determiners e.g. which, whose – to find out which person something belongs to
examplars:
1. Choose a text that illustrates interrogatives.
2. With an example from the text, guide learners to identify the other interrogatives in the passage.
3. Create a scenario for them to use the demonstratives appropriately in sentences.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.3.1: Apply knowledge of different types of pronouns in communication
indicator_description: Identify and use indefinite pronouns e.g. someone, anyone, everything etc. Possessive pronouns to show possession, e.g. mine, ours etc.
examplars:
1. Introduce indefinite pronouns with examples in sentences.
2. Discuss the indefinite pronouns with learners.
3. Provide a passage and group learners to identify indefinite pronouns, and use the pronouns identified in sentences.
4. Repeat the procedure to teach possessive pronouns.
core_competencies: CC, PL, Listening Speaking and Reading

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.4.1: Apply the knowledge of adjectives in communication
indicator_description: Use comparatives forms of regular and irregular adjectives to make comparisons: Regular e.g. shorter; Irregular: better
examplars:
1. Revise the formation of the comparative adjective using er and the superlative using est .e.g. fat, fatter, fattest.
2. Introduce learners to formation of comparison for irregular adjectives.
3. Let them form the comparative forms for irregular adjectives. e.g. good better; Bad worse.
4. Introduce drills to give learners practice in their usage.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.5.1: Apply the knowledge of verbs in communication
indicator_description: Use different types of verbs in sentences: Main verb, Helping verb (primary auxiliary and modal auxiliary)
examplars:
1. Revise verbs using context. Let learners identify main verbs in sentences. E.g. The boy has bought a new football.
2. Provide a text and guide learners to identify auxiliary (Primary and Modal) verbs. E.g. is, was, have, has, had, etc.
3. Create situations and introduce drills for learners to have practice using them.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.5.1: Apply the knowledge of verbs in communication
indicator_description: Use appropriate subject-verb agreement:
examplars:
1. Review subjects and verbs in simple sentences.
2. Use examples to introduce and explain subject-verb agreement.
3. Present sentences with missing subjects and others without verbs for learners to complete.
4. Introduce drills e.g. a completion drill to give further practice.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.5.1: Apply the knowledge of verbs in communication
indicator_description: Use the simple present form of verbs to express: Needs/preferences, Thoughts and ideas
examplars:
1. Revise the simple present form of verbs: - For habitual actions; - For timeless and universal statements; - For facts which may change or hold true indefinitely; - For instantaneous present; - For scheduled future actions.
2. Introduce the simple present form of verbs in sentences to express needs/preferences, and thoughts/ideas.
3. express needs/preferences - I need new clothes. - I like playing football more than volleyball.
4. thoughts/ideas - I think you are right.
5. Introduce these appropriately, one at a time and give practice.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.5.1: Apply the knowledge of verbs in communication
indicator_description: Use the simple past form of verbs to express past needs, interest and feeling:
examplars:
1. Review the simple present form of verbs by asking learners to use them in sentences to express needs/preferences, and thoughts/ideas.
2. Review the simple past form of verbs by using them to indicate: - Completed actions or events - Regular actions in the past
3. Introduce the use of the simple past form of verbs in sentences to express needs, interest and feelings. E.g. Yesterday, I needed some money. I felt his absence badly. I was interested in winning the competition last year.
4. Introduce these in turns and provide activities for practice.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.5.1: Apply the knowledge of verbs in communication
indicator_description: Use irregular form of the simple past tense of verbs, e.g. Irregular – build-built
examplars:
1. Revise regular verbs in simple past forms
2. Introduce the simple past forms of irregular verb in context.
3. In groups, have learners come out with more simple past forms of irregular verbs.
4. Let groups read their verbs to the class.
5. Have learners use the verbs (past forms) in sentences

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.5.1: Apply the knowledge of verbs in communication
indicator_description: Use the past continuous form of verbs to talk about actions/events that were going on when a second action/event took place.
examplars:
1. Introduce the concept by reviewing the present continuous form of verbs.
2. Use the concept in context and have learners identify the time of the actions.
3. Have learners construct similar sentences.
4. Explain that the past continuous form of verbs is used to talk about actions/events that were going on when a second action/event took place.
5. In pairs let learners write sentences using the past continuous form of verbs.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.5.1: Apply the knowledge of verbs in communication
indicator_description: Use the imperative forms of verbs to give instructions and directions
examplars:
1. e.g. Switch on the light. Turn right.
2. Revise commands and requests.
3. Invite pairs of learners to demonstrate giving instructions and directions.
4. Create situations for learners to have practice.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.6.1: Apply the knowledge of different types of adverbs in communication
indicator_description: Use adverbs to express manner
examplars:
1. Revise verbs and adverbs of place and time.
2. Provide a passage with adverbs of manner.
3. Have learners identify the adverbs and their functions – modifying verbs. E.g. Mercy ate slowly.
4. Pair up learners to write short paragraphs using adverbs of manner.
5. Have pairs present their work to the class for feedback.
core_competencies: CC, PL, Listening, Speaking and Reading

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.7.1: Understand and use idiomatic expressions appropriately in speech and in writing
indicator_description: Identify, explain and use idiomatic expressions correctly
examplars:
1. Revise idiomatic expressions such as: i. Take after (to resemble) ii. To pull down(to destroy the reputation of someone)
2. Provide a number of idiomatic expressions and discuss their meaning (in context) with learners.
3. Help learners to interpret given idiomatic expressions in contexts. E.g. i. Kofi takes after his father. ii. They pulled down the Mr. Badu.
4. Have learners use idiomatic expressions in their own sentences.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.8.1: Apply the knowledge of conjunctions in speech and in writing
indicator_description: Identify and use simple conjunctions-because, since, so, although – to give reasons, link contrasting ideas, show results
examplars:
1. Revise simple conjunctions. Provide simple sentences and let learners join them using and, or/nor, but.
2. Introduce because, since, so, although, in context. e.g.: I'm late because I missed the bus. Although Afi is has eye problems, she doesn't wear glasses. He eats well, so he is healthy.
3. Guide learners to discover the functions of these conjunctions.
4. Have them practise using these conjunctions in their own sentences.
5. Provide a passage and have learners identify the conjunctions in the passage.
6. Let learners form sentences and join them with the conjunctions identified.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.9.1: Apply the knowledge of modals in speech and in writing
indicator_description: Use modals to express a variety of meanings: Can, May, Must, Shall/will, Could, Would, Might, Should, Used to, Have to/ought to/need to
examplars:
1. Introduce these (one or two at a time) in context.
2. Use situations and drills to give learners ample practice.

subject: English Language
strand: Grammar and Usage at Word and Phrase Levels
content_standard: B5.3.10.1: Apply the knowledge of prepositions in oral and written communication
indicator_description: Use prepositions to convey a variety of meanings: Position, Direction, Time, Purpose, Possession, Comparison, Support or opposition
examplars:
1. Provide a text and guide learners with examples to identify prepositions used to show different meanings.
2. Let learners construct sentences to convey the following meanings using appropriate prepositions. i. Position – She is standing by the car. ii. Direction – They went towards the East. iii. Time – He left at dawn. iv. Purpose – I bought this hoe for weeding my garden. v. Possession – The shirt is for Kofi. vi. Comparison – She is taller than her brother. vii. Support or opposition – My views are against his plans.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.2.1: Copy and rewrite sentences correctly
indicator_description: Copy sentences clearly in joint script maintaining legible handwriting
examplars:
1. Select sentences from texts learners have read and write them on the board, using joint script.
2. Have learners read out the sentence. (in pairs, small groups, large groups)
3. Have learners copy the sentences into their exercise books.
4. Go round to support struggling learners.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.2.1: Copy and rewrite sentences correctly
indicator_description: Write compound sentences clearly and correctly
examplars:
1. Review simple sentences by asking learners to construct them using given verbs.
2. Construct compound sentences and discuss their structure with learners (they are sentences that have at least two subject and predicates joined by a coordinating conjunction).
3. Give out texts to small groups of learners to search for compound sentences.
4. Have the groups write them into their exercise books.
5. Have individual learners write parallel simple sentences (They should follow the structure of the sentences identified by the groups.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.3.1: Develop, organise and express ideas cohesively in writing and representing for a variety of purposes, audience and context
indicator_description: Choose appropriate ways and modes of writing for a variety of purposes, audiences and contexts and organise facts, ideas and/or points of view in a way appropriate to the mode of delivery, using appropriate text features
examplars:
1. Work with learners to select sample texts from a variety of models of writing from learners' readers and teacher's resources e.g. newspapers, magazines etc. You may take learners to the library for this exercise. Examples of modes of writing. i. Process ii. Narration iii. Description iv. Comparison v. Problem solution vi. Classification vii. Argument. Note: Refer to B4 Strand 4, Sub strand 2, Indicators 1 – 3 for explanation of the modes of writing.
2. Put learners into small groups and assign each group a mode of writing. Note: Present one mode of writing at a time and in different lessons.
3. Have learners study the sample text. Guide them through appropriate questions to identify the: i. Mode of writing/types of essay ii. Purpose iii. Audience
4. Jumble the paragraphs and have learners in groups re-arrange the paragraphs into a coherent piece.
5. Encourage learners to use search engines to search for the different modes of writing they have studied.
6. Discuss the various modes of writing with learners. Have learners read a text to identify the structure of each mode.
core_competencies: CC, PL, Reading and Writing, DL

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.3.1: Develop, organise and express ideas cohesively in writing and representing for a variety of purposes, audience and context
indicator_description: Identify the main idea and minor ideas/supporting details in a paragraph
examplars:
1. Display a Model Paragraph, e.g. The children of Boti go to great lengths to seek the power of education and reading. For example, they build their own school at the beginning of the year! To do this, they use mud to make bricks to build the walls and desks. They use grass and saplings and make a roof. Also, the children of Boti work very hard studying during the school year. Every day they learn something new. Finally, at the end of the year, their minds become fat with knowledge. This is how the children of Boti work hard to seek the power of education and reading.
2. Have learners read it (or read it to them). Have them do Think Pair-Share and answer the questions: i. What is this paragraph about? ii. How do you know?
3. Guide learners towards the idea that the main idea of the paragraph is revealed in the first sentence. Underline the first sentence of the paragraph and write "Topic Sentence" in the margin next to it.
4. Guide learners to identify the details of the paragraph and the sentences that contain these details.
5. Repeat the same process for other sentences.
6. Have learners write their own paragraphs (Guide them to select a topic). Move round to support them.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.3.1: Develop, organise and express ideas cohesively in writing and representing for a variety of purposes, audience and context
indicator_description: Elaborate on, explain and or justify the main ideas of a paragraph by providing explanations and examples
examplars:
1. Display a model paragraph preferably the one above (Refer to B5. 4.3.1.2)
2. By way of revision, have learners identify the main idea and supporting ideas.
3. Think-Pair-Share: Have pairs of learners discuss why a main idea in a paragraph needs elaboration/explanation/examples, and share their views with peers and the whole class.
4. Give learners a topic sentence for them to elaborate on by providing details (explanation/examples). Topic sentence: Kofi is a boy. Details ........................................................ ........................................................

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.3.1: Develop, organise and express ideas cohesively in writing and representing for a variety of purposes, audience and context
indicator_description: Use cohesive devices – pronouns, repetition of vocabulary or grammatical structures to link ideas in a paragraph, e.g. use connectors to link similar ideas, give reasons and add information
examplars:
1. Display a sample paragraph.
2. Underline the cohesive devices/transitional words.
3. Have learners read the paragraph and discuss with their peers what the underlined words are doing in the paragraph.
4. Lead them in a general discussion on what the underlined words are and their role in the paragraph.
5. Provide a paragraph and leave out the cohesive devices. Have learners complete the paragraph with appropriate transitional words.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.9.1: Apply the skills and strategies for idea generation, selection, development, organisation and revision in writing
indicator_description: Select a topic of choice on issues in their community, brainstorm and organise ideas before writing
examplars:
1. Scaffold the writing process. i. Pre-writing
2. Have learners select and discuss topics of interest on issues in their community with their partners.
3. Have learners use strategies such as brainstorming and mind map to generate as many ideas as possible about the topics.
4. Have them organise the ideas into a writing plan using an outline, a chart or appropriate graphic organiser. e.g. line diagram Poor sanitation What is poor sanitation causes/effects solution

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.9.2: Develop and express ideas coherently and cohesively in writing
indicator_description: Develop ideas into a two-paragraph draft without considering the writing conventions, using appropriate linking words within and across paragraphs to aid cohesion and avoid ambiguity, e.g. firstly, then, after
examplars: 1. Put learners into groups to develop their ideas in B5.4.9.1.1 into a three-paragraph draft.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.9.3: Apply strategies for improving drafts for publishing
indicator_description: Review, and revise the draft by proposing grammar for improvement
examplars:
1. Have learners review their first draft by reading it slowly and critically several times to identify words/expressions that need to be cancelled.
2. Guide learners with the questions below: i. Are your ideas in order and easy to understand? ii. Are all the sentences talking about the main idea of the paragraph? iii. Are all the paragraphs talking about the topic?
3. Have learners read their partners' draft and offer suggestions.
4. Encourage learners to feel free to make as many changes as possible to improve their drafts.
5. Have learners make a clean draft for publishing.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.9.3: Apply strategies for improving drafts for publishing
indicator_description: Edit/proofread draft, checking capitalisation, usage of punctuation and spelling
examplars:
1. Have learners pick up their clean draft (Refer to B5 4.9.3.1) and check for conventions. E.g. errors in capitalisation, punctuation and spelling. Tips for learners i. Do my sentences have proper punctuation? E.g. full stop, question mark, exclamation mark. ii. Have I used commas correctly? iii. Do my sentences start with capital letters? iv. Have I capitalised proper nouns?
2. Have learners do peer editing.
3. Have them prepare neat final copies.
4. Guide them to proofread the final copies before publishing.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.9.3: Apply strategies for improving drafts for publishing
indicator_description: Display writing piece for peers to read and publish it in the class magazine
examplars:
1. Have learners share their writing with their peers classmates and families.
2. Encourage learners to copy neatly their writing and submit them for publication in the class magazine, school magazine or notice board, The Junior Graphic

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.10.1: Narrate situations, express feelings and convey point of view about the world/ or fictional world
indicator_description: Create settings, characters and at least one plot in a narrative text, using appropriate linking words within and across paragraphs to aid cohesion, and using simple literary devices, e.g. direct speech
examplars:
1. Revise the stages of the writing process with learners. E.g. prewriting, writing, revising, editing and publishing.
2. Guide learners through the process to write about real or imagined experiences or events. E.g. personal narrative
3. Discuss personal narrative with learners: Note: A personal narrative is a true story told from the writer's point of view. It is the retelling of an event or experience that has affected the writer's life. The story should be told naturally to allow readers to experience the event(s) for themselves.
4. Prewriting: Put learners into groups of five (5) and have them select a topic, purpose and audience for their narrative writing. E.g. Topic: How I spent my Christmas holidays. Purpose: to share an important experience. Audience: Classmates
5. Guide learners to individually use appropriate graphic organizers to generate, gather and organise ideas and details for writing. E.g. Time Line, 5 Ws Chart.
6. Time Line Organiser: Use Time Line for personal narratives to list actions or events in the order in which they occurred. E.g. Topic……… Events in Chronological Order 1. Event 1 2. Event 2 3. Event 3
7. 5 W's Chart: Use 5 W's Chart to collect the who? what? when? where? and why? details for personal narratives and news stories. How could be added to the list.
8. e.g. Topic____________________ Who? What? When? Where? Why?
9. Guide learners to organise their details into writing plant (beginning, middle and ending).
10. Writing stage: Learners then revise their first draft by ensuring the writing has a beginning, middle and ending.
11. Learners should ensure they have used descriptive words. They have written about what they saw, heard, smelled, tasted and touched.
12. Revising: checklist for narrative writing i. Does my essay have beginning, middle and ending? ii. Have I used descriptive words? iii. Are my ideas/events arranged in the order in which they occurred? iv. Does my narrative sound natural? v. Do I express how I feel about what happened?
13. Editing: Have learners use the checklist above to edit their first draft for the conventions of punctuation, capitalisation, spelling and grammar.
14. Publish: Have learners share their work with peers.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.11.1: Create texts
indicator_description: Write freely on topics of choice on issues in the community
examplars:
1. Revise the stages of the writing process
2. Have learners follow the writing process to create their own pieces on a given topic.
3. Learners present their work for editing.
4. Have pupils publish their works on the notice board.
core_competencies: CC, CP, Reading and Writing, CI

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.11.1: Create texts
indicator_description: Write poems and imaginative narrative stories using knowledge of features of poems and imaginative texts
examplars:
1. Discuss elements of narrative stories with learners. - Title - Characters - Setting - Plot/story line - Conflict dialogue etc. Note: Stories have people (characters) who face a problem (conflict) in a particular place and time (setting). They show what people do (action) and say (dialogue) to resolve the problem.
2. Guide learners through the writing process to write their stories.
3. Put pupils into small groups to plan their writing. - Create characters - Create conflict - Establish a setting - Plan action and dialogue
4. Have learners write the first draft, revise, edit and publish their writing. Refer to Strand 4, Sub-strand for the details of the stages of the writing process.
5. Poems and Plays: Discuss sentences of poems and plays. Guide learners through the writing process to write poems and plays.
core_competencies: CC, CP, Reading and Writing, CI

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.12.1: Demonstrate knowledge of descriptive words/expressions in writing
indicator_description: Use descriptive words/expressions/sound devices/ figurative language to describe places, personal experiences and events.
examplars:
1. Revise descriptive writing with learners.
2. Talk about sensory details that allow a reader to visualise a person, a place, a thing or an idea.
3. Guide learners through the stages of writing.
4. Write to describe events/situations/places of their choice and personal experiences. Focus on the use of: - Descriptive (adjectives) - Figurative language e.g. simile – metaphor - Sensory details (sensory details that are experience through the senses: sight, smell, touch, taste

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.13.1: Support an opinion in writing
indicator_description: Write explanations to support opinions
examplars:
1. Present a motion to learners. E.g. A teacher is more important than a doctor.
2. Discuss the meaning and implication of the motion with pupils and invite them to take a stand either for or against the motion.
3. Put learners into two groups: for the motion and against the motion to write down what they think about it.
4. Groups read out their opinions to the class for discussion.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.13.2: Write arguments to support claims with clear reasons and relevant evidence
indicator_description: Introduce claims and support them with clear reasons and evidence using credible sources
examplars:
1. Working in groups (as in B5.7.1.1), learners provide reasons for the opinions they hold on a topic.
2. Guide groups to provide evidence for the opinions they hold.
3. Have groups present their work for discussions.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.13.2: Write arguments to support claims with clear reasons and relevant evidence
indicator_description: Use words, phrases, and clauses to clarify the relationships between claim(s) and reasons
examplars:
1. Lead learners to discuss appropriate language for arguments e. g. use of adjectives in their various forms.
2. You may read out models of argumentative essays to guide learners.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.13.2: Write arguments to support claims with clear reasons and relevant evidence
indicator_description: Establish and maintain a formal style
examplars:
1. Using models, discuss the basic structure of an argumentative piece: - Introduction. - Reasons for the stand taken. - Conclusion.
2. Have groups present full compositions using this structure for class discussions and feedback.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.13.2: Write arguments to support claims with clear reasons and relevant evidence
indicator_description: Provide a concluding statement that follows from the arguments presented
examplars:
1. Revise persuasive writing.
2. Have them go through the writing process to present/state an opinion, explain and justify it so as to persuade the reader to accept their opinion or point of view.
3. Teach the features of a debate.
4. Guide learners to select a controversial or debatable topic. Divide the class into two to prepare using the writing process as a guide, and debate on the topic.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.14.1: Write informative/ expository essays
indicator_description: Write sentences to describe a process of doing something
examplars:
1. Use pictures showing the stages of how some dishes are prepared.
2. Learners in groups observe the picture sequences and write words that give information on the activity.
3. Each group presents its work for discussion.
4. Let learners in groups choose a domestic activity they would want to give information on. (They should be able to compose expository pieces of between 90 and 120 words).

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.14.2: Write the event of the day
indicator_description: Write about incidence or events of the day (e.g. any event witnessed on their way to school) and make radio/TV presentations
examplars:
1. Let learners watch TV news at home and write their own news from the pictures they see.
2. Put learners into groups. Let them brainstorm and write about events of the day. Each group chooses an interesting event and writes the news to broadcast to the class. Let each group choose one person to present the news.
3. Have learners publish their works on the internet.
4. Help learners to organise a radio or talk show

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.4.15.1: Write informal letters on given topics
indicator_description: Write to friends about events using appropriate letter formats
examplars:
1. In groups learners are given samples of friendly letters.
2. Let them brainstorm and write the important features of the letter and other special things they identify in the letters.
3. Learners present their information to the whole class to guide the class to learn about such letters.
4. Guide learners in their groups to choose an imaginary friend they want to write to.
5. Learners brainstorm and write down ideas on what they would want to include in their letters.
6. Guide them to write their letters and let them edit their work.
7. Learners exchange their product with other groups to edit.
8. Learners read their letters to others.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.5.1.1: Use capital letters to start the first words in a direct speech
indicator_description: Follow appropriate mechanical convention
examplars: 1. Ask individual learners to write a formal letter each on a given topic paying attention to correct use of capital letters, the full stop, comma, question mark, exclamation mark, quotation mark, apostrophe, hyphen etc.

subject: English Language
content_standard: B5.5.1.1: Use capital letters to start the first words in a direct speech
indicator_description: Writing of lower case and capital letters.
examplars:
1. Guide learners to write about their friends by reading a sample essay on the topic to them.
2. Guide learners to write one paragraph about their friends after listening to the teacher read a sample essay on the topic to them.
3. Learners in pairs plan their writing.
4. Let individuals write about their friends. Learners exchange their work with their partners and guide them to edit.
